source_paper_title: Tightness Without Counterexamples: A New Approach and New Results for Prophet Inequalities
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Jiang_2025, title={Tightness Without Counterexamples: A New Approach and New Results for Prophet Inequalities}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0221}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0221}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Jiang, Jiashuo and Ma, Will and Zhang, Jiawei}, year={2025}, month=apr }
context:
Page 6, Introduction, item 2 under “1.2. New Results”.

It remains a challenging problem to analytically verify for all \(k > 1\) our numerical observation that \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \gamma_{k,n} = \inf_{n\ge 1} \gamma_{k,n}\), which would resolve the open question of k-unit IID prophet inequality.
title: Analytically prove equality of asymptotic and worst-case IID k-unit prophet ratios
background_latex:
Consider the IID multi-unit prophet inequality with a uniform matroid (capacity) constraint. There are integers \(k\ge 2\) (number of selection slots) and \(n\ge 1\) (number of sequentially arriving agents). Each arriving agent \(i\in\{1,\dots,n\}\) reveals a nonnegative random value \(R_i\) upon arrival; the \(R_i\) are independent and identically distributed (IID) according to a known distribution. An online policy must irrevocably accept or reject each agent upon arrival, may accept at most \(k\) agents in total, and aims to maximize expected total accepted value \(\mathbb{E}[\sum_{i\in S} R_i]\), where \(|S|\le k\) is the (random) accepted set.

Let \(\mathrm{DP}(I)\) denote the expected value of an optimal adaptive online policy (equivalently, the optimal dynamic program) on instance \(I\). Let \(\mathrm{Proph}(I)\) denote the prophet benchmark, defined as the expected sum of the \(k\) largest realized values among \(R_1,\dots,R_n\), i.e.
\[\mathrm{Proph}(I)=\mathbb{E}\Big[\max_{S\subseteq \{1,\dots,n\},\ |S|\le k}\ \sum_{i\in S} R_i\Big].\]
For fixed \(k\) and \(n\), define the tight IID prophet-inequality guarantee
\[\gamma_{k,n} := \inf_{I\in \mathcal{I}^{\mathrm{IID}}_{k,n}} \frac{\mathrm{DP}(I)}{\mathrm{Proph}(I)},\]
where \(\mathcal{I}^{\mathrm{IID}}_{k,n}\) denotes the class of all IID instances with \(k\) slots and \(n\) agents (over all nonnegative value distributions).

Two natural aggregations over \(n\) are:
1) the asymptotic-in-\(n\) limit \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \gamma_{k,n}\) (when it exists), and
2) the worst case over all horizons \(\inf_{n\ge 1} \gamma_{k,n}\).

For \(k=1\), classical work characterizes \(\gamma_{1,n}\) and shows the worst case occurs as \(n\to\infty\). For \(k>1\), the paper reports numerical evidence about the relationship between \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}\) and \(\inf_{n\ge 1}\gamma_{k,n}\), but does not give an analytic proof for all \(k>1\).
problem_definition_latex:
For each integer \(k>1\), establish analytically that
\[\lim_{n\to\infty} \gamma_{k,n} = \inf_{n\ge 1} \gamma_{k,n},\]
where \(\gamma_{k,n} := \inf_{I\in \mathcal{I}^{\mathrm{IID}}_{k,n}} \mathrm{DP}(I)/\mathrm{Proph}(I)\) is the tight performance guarantee of the optimal adaptive online policy relative to the prophet benchmark over IID instances with \(k\) slots and \(n\) agents.
latex:
Consider the IID multi-unit prophet inequality with a uniform matroid (capacity) constraint. There are integers \(k\ge 2\) (number of selection slots) and \(n\ge 1\) (number of sequentially arriving agents). Each arriving agent \(i\in\{1,\dots,n\}\) reveals a nonnegative random value \(R_i\) upon arrival; the \(R_i\) are independent and identically distributed (IID) according to a known distribution. An online policy must irrevocably accept or reject each agent upon arrival, may accept at most \(k\) agents in total, and aims to maximize expected total accepted value \(\mathbb{E}[\sum_{i\in S} R_i]\), where \(|S|\le k\) is the (random) accepted set.

Let \(\mathrm{DP}(I)\) denote the expected value of an optimal adaptive online policy (equivalently, the optimal dynamic program) on instance \(I\). Let \(\mathrm{Proph}(I)\) denote the prophet benchmark, defined as the expected sum of the \(k\) largest realized values among \(R_1,\dots,R_n\), i.e.
\[\mathrm{Proph}(I)=\mathbb{E}\Big[\max_{S\subseteq \{1,\dots,n\},\ |S|\le k}\ \sum_{i\in S} R_i\Big].\]
For fixed \(k\) and \(n\), define the tight IID prophet-inequality guarantee
\[\gamma_{k,n} := \inf_{I\in \mathcal{I}^{\mathrm{IID}}_{k,n}} \frac{\mathrm{DP}(I)}{\mathrm{Proph}(I)},\]
where \(\mathcal{I}^{\mathrm{IID}}_{k,n}\) denotes the class of all IID instances with \(k\) slots and \(n\) agents (over all nonnegative value distributions).

Two natural aggregations over \(n\) are:
1) the asymptotic-in-\(n\) limit \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \gamma_{k,n}\) (when it exists), and
2) the worst case over all horizons \(\inf_{n\ge 1} \gamma_{k,n}\).

For \(k=1\), classical work characterizes \(\gamma_{1,n}\) and shows the worst case occurs as \(n\to\infty\). For \(k>1\), the paper reports numerical evidence about the relationship between \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}\) and \(\inf_{n\ge 1}\gamma_{k,n}\), but does not give an analytic proof for all \(k>1\).

For each integer \(k>1\), establish analytically that
\[\lim_{n\to\infty} \gamma_{k,n} = \inf_{n\ge 1} \gamma_{k,n},\]
where \(\gamma_{k,n} := \inf_{I\in \mathcal{I}^{\mathrm{IID}}_{k,n}} \mathrm{DP}(I)/\mathrm{Proph}(I)\) is the tight performance guarantee of the optimal adaptive online policy relative to the prophet benchmark over IID instances with \(k\) slots and \(n\) agents.
location_in_paper: Page 6, Introduction, item 2 under “1.2. New Results”.
open_problem_quote: It remains a challenging problem to analytically verify for all \(k > 1\) our numerical observation that \(\lim_{n\to\infty} \gamma_{k,n} = \inf_{n\ge 1} \gamma_{k,n}\), which would resolve the open question of k-unit IID prophet inequality.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is an equality between a limit and an infimum over all horizons and all IID distributions, so falsifying it would require searching over a very large (infinite-dimensional) space of value distributions and proving optimality gaps; finite instance searches give at best suggestive evidence.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can numerically approximate \(\gamma_{k,n}\) via the paper’s semi-infinite LP discretization for many \((k,n)\) and attempt to detect candidate worst-case horizons or distributions, providing strong experimental guidance. However, turning this into an analytic proof of the equality for all \(k>1\) likely requires new theoretical ideas beyond automated search.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
Jiang, Ma, and Zhang (2025) formulate the IID \(k\)-unit prophet ratio \(\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_{F}\mathrm{DP}(F,n)/\mathrm{Proph}(F,n)\) via a semi-infinite LP in quantile space (their iidLP\(_{k,n}^{\mathrm{DP/Proph}}\)), obtained by dualizing an inner LP over value gaps \(\Delta_j\) and interpreting the dual as a 
“Type Coverage” problem. In the IID case, the outer minimization over type distributions collapses to an infinitely granular quantile model, yielding constraints indexed by \(q\in(0,1]\) of the form \(\theta\,\mathbb{E}[\min\{\mathrm{Bin}(n,q),k\}]\le \sum_{i,l}\min\{y_i^l,qx_i^l\}\). This gives a principled way to compute \(\gamma_{k,n}\) numerically for fixed \(k,n\), and it recovers the classical \(k=1\) limit constant \(\theta^*\approx 0.745\) by analyzing a relaxation and proving a doubling-horizon monotonicity for that relaxation. However, for \(k>1\) the paper leaves open the analytic relationship between the asymptotic limit \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}\) and the worst-case-over-horizons value \(\inf_{n\ge 1}\gamma_{k,n}\), reporting numerical evidence that they coincide.

The most directly relevant forward progress is Brustle, Perez-Salazar, and Verdugo’s 
“Splitting guarantees for prophet inequalities via nonlinear systems,” which also targets the i.i.d. \((k,n)\)-selection prophet ratio and provides an exact infinite-dimensional LP characterization for each \(n\), together with a limiting nonlinear ODE system \(\mathrm{NLS}_k\) that yields asymptotic lower bounds for large \(n\). This strengthens the analytical toolkit around the \(n\to\infty\) regime and clarifies how Poisson/ODE limits emerge from the discrete LPs, but it does not establish that the infimum over all horizons is achieved in the limit, nor a monotonicity principle in \(n\) for \(\gamma_{k,n}\).

A parallel line of partial progress appears in work on related benchmarks and restricted policy classes. 
“Prophet Inequalities: Competing with the Top \(\ell\) Items is Easy” proves a horizon-doubling monotonicity and hence \(\inf_n=\liminf_{n\to\infty}\) for a single-choice \(k=1\) setting (with an average-of-top-\(\ell\) benchmark), and develops ODE limits for multi-choice but leaves the horizon-worst-case question open for \(k>1\). Meanwhile, tight Poisson-limit worst-case phenomena are known for OCRS/magician-style problems relative to ex-ante relaxations (e.g., Jiang–Ma–Zhang 2022 and stationary OCRS work), suggesting a plausible route: prove a suitable horizon-doubling (or more general) monotonicity for the IID Type Coverage LP/dual, or show that any finite-horizon worst-case instance can be embedded into a longer horizon without increasing \(\mathrm{DP}/\mathrm{Proph}\). At present, no cited work provides such an embedding/monotonicity argument for the full IID \(k\)-unit prophet ratio against the prophet benchmark, so the equality \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_{n\ge 1}\gamma_{k,n}\) remains open for \(k>1\).

sources.title: Splitting guarantees for prophet inequalities via nonlinear systems
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper gives (i) an exact infinite-dimensional LP characterization of the worst-case i.i.d. 
(k,n)-selection prophet ratio \(\gamma_{k,n}\) (Theorem 1: \(\gamma_{k,n}\) equals the optimum of LP \([P]_{n,k}\)), and (ii) for each fixed \(k\) a nonlinear ODE system \(\mathrm{NLS}_k(\theta)\) with constants \(\theta_1,\dots,\theta_k\) such that for some \(n_0(k)\) and all \(n\ge n_0(k)\),
\[\gamma_{k,n}\ge \Big(1-\tfrac{24k\ln^2 n}{n}\Big)\sum_{j=1}^k \theta_j^\star,\]
where \(\theta^\star\) is a parameter vector for which \(\mathrm{NLS}_k(\theta^\star)\) has a solution (Theorem 2).
sources.connection: The open problem asks to prove analytically, for each \(k>1\), that the worst-case over horizons equals the asymptotic-in-\(n\) limit: \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_{n\ge1}\gamma_{k,n}\). This paper does not establish that equality. It develops tools to study \(\gamma_{k,n}\) for every \(n\) (exact LP) and derives asymptotic lower bounds for large \(n\) via a limiting nonlinear differential system. These results are directly relevant to analyzing the asymptotic behavior \(\liminf_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}\) and to constructing near-asymptotically optimal policies, but they do not prove that the infimum over all \(n\) is attained in the limit \(n\to\infty\), nor any monotonicity/limit-exchange property needed for the equality. In fact, the paper explicitly states that finding an analytical formula for \(\gamma_{k,n}\) and extending results to all \(n\) remain open, and it leaves open whether \(\inf_{n\ge1}\gamma_{k,n}=\sum_j\theta_j^\star\).

sources.title: Prophet Inequalities: Competing with the Top  Items is Easy
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves for the single-selection i.i.d. prophet problem with benchmark the average of the top \(\ell\) order statistics that the worst-case competitive ratio \(\mathrm{CR}_\ell:=\inf_{n\ge \ell}\inf_F \sup_	au \mathbb{E}[X_	au]/\mathbb{E}[ rac1\ell\sum_{i=1}^\ell X_{(i)}]\) equals the unique solution \(\mathrm{CR}_\ell\in[0,1)\) of the integral equation
\[ rac{1}{\ell!}\int_0^\infty 
u^{\ell-1} e^{
u(1/\mathrm{CR}_\ell-1)} + \sum_{i=0}^\ell  rac{
u^i}{i!}\, d
u = 1.\]
It also proves a horizon-monotonicity property for this \(k=1\) setting: \(\mathrm{CR}_\ell(2n)\le \mathrm{CR}_\ell(n)\), hence \(\mathrm{CR}_\ell=\inf_n \mathrm{CR}_\ell(n)=\liminf_{n	o\infty}\mathrm{CR}_\ell(n).\) For the multi-selection case, it gives asymptotic lower bounds \(\liminf_{n	o\infty}\mathrm{CR}_{k,\ell}(n)\ge  rac{\mathrm{CR}_\ell}{k}\sum_{j=1}^k \prod_{t=1}^j 	heta_{t,\ell}^{-1}\) where \(	heta_{t,\ell}\) are defined via a boundary-value ODE system.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks, for the i.i.d. \(k\)-unit prophet inequality with uniform matroid constraint, to prove analytically that the worst-case ratio over horizons equals the asymptotic-in-\(n\) limit: \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_n\gamma_{k,n}\) for each \(k>1\), where \(\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_F \mathrm{DP}(F,n)/\mathrm{Proph}(F,n)\) and the prophet benchmark is the expected sum of the top \(k\) values.
This paper does not establish that equality for \(k>1\). It proves an analogous ‘worst case at large \(n\)’ statement only for the single-choice case \(k=1\) (Lemma 3.1 implies \(\mathrm{CR}_\ell(2n)\le \mathrm{CR}_\ell(n)\) and thus the infimum over \(n\) is achieved asymptotically). For \(k>1\) it explicitly states it lacks a proof that \(\mathrm{CR}_{k,\ell}=\liminf_{n\to\infty}\mathrm{CR}_{k,\ell}(n)\), and provides only asymptotic lower bounds via an ODE system. Moreover, its main benchmark is the average of top \(\ell\) items (and general \((k,\ell)\) framework), not exactly the \(k\)-unit prophet ratio \(\mathrm{DP}/\mathrm{Proph}\) with \(\ell=k\) and sum (not average) benchmark—though these are equivalent up to scaling when \(\ell=k\). Thus it gives partial progress: it settles the ‘worst case at \(n\to\infty\)’ phenomenon for \(k=1\) and develops ODE/quantile machinery relevant to the \(k>1\) question, but does not solve it.

sources.title: Prophet Inequalities: Competing with the Top Items is Easy
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves that for the single-choice i.i.d. prophet problem with benchmark equal to the average of the top \(\ell\) order statistics, the worst-case competitive ratio \(\mathrm{CR}_\ell:=\inf_{n\ge \ell}\inf_F \sup_	au \mathbb{E}[X_	au]/\mathbb{E}[ rac1\ell\sum_{i=1}^\ell X_{(i)}]\) equals the unique solution \(\mathrm{CR}_\ell\in[0,1)\) of the integral equation
\[ rac{1}{\ell!}\int_0^\infty 
u^{\ell-1} e^{
u(1/\mathrm{CR}_\ell-1)} + \sum_{i=0}^\ell  rac{
u^i}{i!}\, d
u = 1.\]
It also proves a horizon-monotonicity property for this single-choice setting: \(\mathrm{CR}_\ell=\inf_{n\ge \ell}\mathrm{CR}_\ell(n)=\liminf_{n	o\infty}\mathrm{CR}_\ell(n)\) (Lemma 3.1), and gives asymptotic lower bounds for the multi-choice setting \(\mathrm{CR}_{k,\ell}(n)\) as \(n	o\infty\) via a system of ODEs (Theorem 1.3).
sources.connection: The open problem asks, for the i.i.d. \(k\)-unit prophet inequality with uniform matroid capacity \(k>1\), to prove analytically that the worst-case over horizons \(\inf_{n\ge1}\gamma_{k,n}\) equals the asymptotic limit \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}\). This paper establishes exactly that kind of ‘worst case at large horizon’ statement only for the single-choice case (their Lemma 3.1, but with a different benchmark: average of top \(\ell\) items). For \(k>1\) it provides only asymptotic (in \(n\)) lower bounds (Theorem 1.3) and explicitly states it does not prove \(\mathrm{CR}_{k,\ell}=\liminf_{n\to\infty}\mathrm{CR}_{k,\ell}(n)\) nor asymptotic tightness. Thus it does not resolve the equality \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_n\gamma_{k,n}\) for multi-unit \(k>1\) with the prophet benchmark (sum of top \(k\)). Still, its techniques (doubling-horizon hardness transformation for \(k=1\), ODE limits from discrete boundary value problems) are closely related and may inform an approach for \(k>1\).

sources.title: Tight Guarantees for Multi-unit Prophet Inequalities and Online Stochastic Knapsack
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper characterizes the tight worst-case approximation ratio (over all horizons and independent—possibly non-IID—arrivals) of k-unit prophet inequalities relative to an LP relaxation: it defines a tight constant \(\gamma_k^*\) (computed via an ODE arising from a Poisson limit) such that a \(\gamma\)-Conservative Magician/OCRS achieves ratio \(\gamma_k^*\), and no online algorithm can exceed \(\gamma_k^*\) against the LP benchmark. In particular, the worst case for the k-unit OCRS occurs in the limit of many tiny activation probabilities \(p_t=k/T\) with \(T\to\infty\), yielding a Poisson process of rate \(k\) (Theorem 2).
sources.connection: The open problem asks, in the IID k-unit prophet setting with prophet benchmark \(\mathrm{Proph}(I)=\mathbb{E}[\text{sum of top-}k]\), to prove that the worst-case ratio over horizons \(\inf_n \gamma_{k,n}\) equals the asymptotic limit \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}\). This paper does not analyze \(\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_I \mathrm{DP}(I)/\mathrm{Proph}(I)\) in the IID model; instead it studies a stronger/adversarial model and a different denominator (LP relaxation \(UP(H)\)), proving the worst case for its OCRS/LP ratio occurs in a Poisson limit (many small \(p_t\)). While this ‘Poisson limit is worst-case’ phenomenon is conceptually similar to ‘worst case occurs as \(n\to\infty\)’, it does not directly establish the equality of \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}\) and \(\inf_n\gamma_{k,n}\) for the IID prophet benchmark. At best, it provides techniques (LP/duality, splitting argument, Poisson limiting worst case) that might be adaptable to the IID-prophet-ratio question.

sources.title: Stationary Online Contention Resolution Schemes
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper introduces stationary online contention resolution schemes (S-OCRSs), gives an exact LP characterization of stationarity, and a universal “simulate-then-replace” implementation. For the k-uniform matroid (capacity k), it constructs an optimal stationary OCRS with selectability
\[\alpha_k := \Pr[Q<k\mid Q\le k] = \frac{\Pr[Q<k]}{\Pr[Q\le k]},\quad Q\sim \mathrm{Poisson}(k),\]
with asymptotic expansion \(\alpha_k = 1-\sqrt{2/(\pi k)}+O(1/k)\), and proves \(\alpha_k\) is the best possible selectability among stationary OCRSs for k-uniform matroids (Theorem 3 and Lemma 5).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for an analytic proof that, for IID k-unit prophet inequalities under a uniform matroid constraint, the worst-case ratio over horizons n equals the asymptotic-in-n limit: \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_{n\ge1}\gamma_{k,n}\). This paper does not study \(\gamma_{k,n}\) (DP vs prophet benchmark) as a function of n, nor does it compare finite-n and asymptotic-n worst cases. Instead it studies OCRSs (a rounding primitive) and proves optimal stationary selectability constants for k-uniform matroids, expressed via truncated Poisson probabilities. These results are adjacent: OCRSs are connected to prophet inequalities via ex-ante relaxations, and the Poisson/truncation analysis resembles asymptotic worst-case phenomena in k-selection. However, the paper does not provide the requested equality between \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}\) and \(\inf_n\gamma_{k,n}\) for the IID prophet ratio, nor an argument that the worst case occurs as n→∞.

sources.title: Competition Versus Complexity in Multiple-Selection Prophet Inequalities
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper studies a different objective: competition complexity for single-threshold algorithms in the IID multi-unit prophet setting. It gives an exact formula for the worst-case ratio of the best single-threshold algorithm with horizon \(m\) against a prophet with horizon \(n\):
\[\gamma_{m,n,k}=\inf_F\sup_q \frac{\mathrm{ALG}_{m,k}(F,q)}{\mathrm{OPT}_{n,k}(F)}=\frac{Q_{m,k}(k/n)}{k},\]
where \(Q_{m,k}(q)=\mathbb{E}[\min\{k,Y\}]\) for \(Y\sim\mathrm{Binomial}(m,q)\). It also characterizes the \((1-\varepsilon)\)-competition complexity \(\beta_k(\varepsilon)\), with \(\beta_1(\varepsilon)=\ln(1/\varepsilon)\) and for \(k>1\),
\[\frac{\ln(1/\varepsilon)}{k}\le \beta_k(\varepsilon)\le 1+\frac{2\ln(1/\varepsilon)}{k-1}+\sqrt{\frac{2\ln(1/\varepsilon)}{k-1}}.\]
sources.connection:
The open problem asks to prove for the *optimal adaptive online policy* (dynamic program) that the worst-case IID \(k\)-unit prophet ratio over horizons \(n\) is achieved asymptotically, i.e. \(\lim_{n\to\infty}\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_n\gamma_{k,n}\), where \(\gamma_{k,n}=\inf_I \mathrm{DP}(I)/\mathrm{Proph}(I)\).

This paper does not analyze \(\gamma_{k,n}\) for the optimal DP policy. Instead it analyzes a different ratio \(\gamma_{m,n,k}\) for the restricted class of single-threshold policies, and moreover compares an online algorithm with \(m\) samples to a prophet with \(n\) samples (competition complexity). While its infinite-dimensional LP/duality techniques in quantile space are conceptually related to the LP approaches used in prophet-inequality tightness work, it does not address whether the worst-case over \(n\) for the DP ratio occurs at \(n\to\infty\), nor does it provide monotonicity/limit arguments for \(\gamma_{k,n}\).
